turns.content: man I am dragging today. its not even noon and I feel like I could lay down on the shop floor and pass out. what are some good foods for energy that actually work

That midday crash is brutal. Here are some solid energy foods that actually hold up and aren't just sugar spikes:

- **Nuts** — almonds, walnuts, cashews. Easy to keep in the shop, steady energy.
- **Hard-boiled eggs** — cheap, filling, protein-heavy. Make a batch on Sunday.
- **Bananas + peanut butter** — quick, portable, good combo of carbs and fat.
- **Greek yogurt** — more protein than regular yogurt, keeps you going.
- **Oatmeal** — if you can do a morning bowl, it's slow-burning fuel.

The big thing is avoiding the stuff that spikes you and drops you — white bread, sugary drinks, candy bars, fast food combos.

What does your eating look like right now? Are you skipping meals or grabbing whatever's fast?

honestly I had a gas station biscuit this morning and a mountain dew. I know thats terrible. my blood sugar was all over the place this morning too. I got type 2 a few years back and some days the metformin just isnt enough you know

Yeah, that combination is working against you hard — especially with Type 2 diabetes. A biscuit and Mountain Dew is basically a pure sugar bomb. Your blood sugar spikes, the metformin is fighting to bring it down, and then you crash. That's exactly why you feel like you're dying before noon.

With Type 2, what you eat matters even more than it does for most people. The metformin can only do so much if the food is constantly spiking you.

Here's a realistic swap that won't require you to become a chef:

**Instead of the gas station biscuit:**
- Hard-boiled eggs (make 6 on Sunday, grab 2 each morning)
- Or a handful of almonds and a cheese stick
- Or overnight oats (literally dump oats, milk, and peanut butter in a jar the night before)

**Instead of Mountain Dew:**
- Black coffee or coffee with a little cream
- Water with a squeeze of lemon
- If you need fizz, diet or zero-sugar versions (not great but miles better)

That one swap alone will probably change how you feel by noon. Are you tracking your blood sugar regularly or just when it feels off?
I check it in the morning most days. some days I forget. my doctor keeps getting on me about it

Your doctor's right on that one. Checking in the morning is good, but it'd help to also check about 2 hours after a meal — that's when you'd see the spike from something like that biscuit. It tells you which foods are actually hitting you hardest.

Keep a sticky note on your meter or set a phone alarm to remind you. Even checking twice a day — morning and one post-meal — gives you way more information to work with.

But honestly, the biggest bang for your buck right now is fixing the breakfast. That gas station stop is probably setting the tone for your whole day. If you get protein and fat in the morning instead of sugar and carbs, your afternoon energy will be night and day different.
the hard boiled egg thing is probably the most realistic for me. I can do that. I aint making overnight oats or whatever that is

Ha, fair enough. Eggs it is. Here's your dead-simple system:

- Sunday night: boil a dozen eggs, put them in the fridge
- Each morning: grab 2-3, throw them in a ziplock, eat them at the shop
- Keep a jar of almonds or peanuts at your desk for when you need a snack later

That's it. No cooking during the week, no prep, 10 minutes on Sunday. And the protein will keep your blood sugar way more stable than what you're doing now.

For the Mountain Dew situation — try switching to coffee or even just water for a week and see how you feel. The sugar in that soda is probably the single biggest thing spiking you.
the mountain dew is gonna be the hard one to quit. I been drinking those since I was a kid

I hear you. That's a lifelong habit — don't try to go cold turkey or you'll be right back to it by Wednesday.

Step it down:
- **Week 1:** Switch to Mountain Dew Zero or Diet. Still tastes close, no sugar spike. Not health food, but it removes the blood sugar problem.
- **Week 2:** Cut down to one a day instead of however many you're drinking.
- **Week 3+:** Start replacing with coffee or water when you can.

The goal isn't perfection. It's getting your blood sugar to stop going on a roller coaster every morning. Even just switching to the zero-sugar version is a massive win for someone managing Type 2. Your meter will show you the difference in a few days.
I can try the zero sugar one. my buddy drinks those and says they taste almost the same

They're pretty close honestly. Most people adjust in a day or two. And when you see your blood sugar numbers start leveling out, that'll be all the motivation you need to stick with it.

So your game plan:
1. Boil eggs Sunday night
2. Swap to Mountain Dew Zero
3. Keep almonds or peanuts at the shop

Three changes. That's it. Give it one week and I bet you're not face-down on the shop floor at noon anymore.
I tell you what if I can get through a day without wanting to nap at 2pm that would be a win. appreciate the help man

That 2pm crash should get a lot better with these changes. Your body's been running on sugar spikes and crashes — once you give it actual fuel, it's a different ballgame.

Give it a week and see how the numbers look on your meter. If you're still dragging after cleaning up the food, might be worth mentioning to your doctor at the next visit — could be a dosage thing.

Good luck with it. Boil those eggs Sunday.
